summary: The post is a March 2019 status report from Ursa Labs centered on shipping Apache Arrow 0.13.0 and the infrastructure needed to sustain it. McKinney highlights work on build and test systems, core C++ architecture, and packaging across languages as prerequisites for reliable, scalable development. He frames progress as a mix of foundational engineering (build revamp, expression algebra, query engine design) and ecosystem enablement (Flight, R packaging, Gandiva distribution). The post concludes with gratitude to sponsors and a detailed changelog that signals steady, broad-based momentum. Overall, the argument is that durable data infrastructure requires coordinated, cross-cutting engineering and community collaboration.
key_points: The release focus drove substantial effort on build, test, and packaging infrastructure to keep Arrow development scalable and reliable.
Major C++ work advanced core capabilities like expression algebra and laid groundwork for an embeddable query engine.
Arrow Flight and related tooling progressed through improved dependencies and collaboration with industry partners.
Cross-language support (R, Python, Gandiva, conda-forge) is treated as essential for adoption and stability.
The volume of merged commits and detailed changelog underscores sustained, coordinated progress across the project.
money_quotes: The first quarter of 2019 has now wrapped up.
In March we spent a good amount of time focused on getting the 0.13.0 Apache Arrow release out of the door.
We are continuing to set up our physical build and test cluster.
We have begun prototyping an expression algebra to use for query engine development in C++.
We have been working closely with Two Sigma and Dremio on this important effort.
We are working to get the Arrow R package submitted to CRAN for R users.
We have much work ahead of us and look forward to seeing you on GitHub, JIRA, and the dev@arrow.apache.org developer mailing list.
key_insight: Sustaining Arrow’s growth requires deep infrastructure work across builds, packaging, and core C++ capabilities, coordinated with a broad open-source community.
